data.title: Cofinimmo SA 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cofinimmo SA in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Italian Confidence Edges Higher As Modest Growth Path Holds Firm
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli

Consumers more upbeat in July

Italian consumer confidence improved in July, with most components posting monthly gains except expectations of future economic developments. Interestingly, this did not impact expectations of future unemployment, which remained stable at the June level, confirming past declines. A steady belief in labour market resilience is reflected in improved expectations of household balance sheets and rising intentions of purchasing durable goods. If confirmed, these indications might point to stronger consumption over the summer months. Interestingly, this seems consistent with the improvement in confidence among retailers, driven by better sales expectations.

Signs of improvement in the manufacturing domain confirmed in July

The picture is also slowly improving among manufacturers. Here, confidence saw a fourth consecutive monthly gain, with a slight decline in inventories and a timid increase in orders, mirrored in improving production expectations and possibly hinting at the start of an inventory cycle. This seems more evident among producers of investment goods, who are reaping the benefits of recovery and resilience fund spending. Despite the lingering uncertainty related to the US tariff saga, the stabilisation of manufacturing is now morphing into a slow prospective recovery, which will presumably be fuelled by stronger demand originating from the substantial German investment plan.

Construction confidence softer, with mixed signals from components

On a less positive note, confidence had a modest setback in the construction sector. Unsurprisingly, the fall was mainly driven by a decline in the residential construction component, which had proved surprisingly resilient over the past few months despite the phasing out of the generous Superbonus tax incentive. The infrastructural component is holding up well, though, fuelled by the implementation of investments funded by EU recovery funds.

Confidence softer also in services, within a volatile downward path

Confidence data came in on the weak side among market service providers, with the exception of tourism services. Confidence in the service sector has been somewhat volatile over the last few months, with a downward trend suggesting that the sector cannot indefinitely bear the brunt of economic growth in isolation. Tourism itself is showing some signs of fatigue, particularly in the domestic component.

July confidence data still consistent with low but positive GDP growth in the third quarter

All in all, today’s confidence data releases point to a continuation of the low growth path for the Italian economy in the third quarter. Consumption might in principle play a bigger role, given labour market resilience, decent wage growth, and sub-2% inflation. The investment front will still reflect an infrastructural push and a residential drag, with developments in the machinery part very much linked to the end point of the tariff saga.

Should this prove bearable, the reduction in uncertainty would likely translate into a recovery in this component over the last part of 2025. We still believe that the Italian economy will post only marginal growth rates over the second and third quarters and accelerate slightly in the fourth, but we also acknowledge an upward risk emerging if a trade deal with the US is reached soon.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Intel: Turnaround Risks Vs. Upside Potential
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) reported weaker-than-expected earnings for its second fiscal quarter as the chipmaker was hit again once again by restructuring costs and weighed down by a slow-moving reorganization. Intel also generated no revenue growth in the second quarter, on a year-over-year basis, and submitted a disappointing outlook for the third quarter with EPS. Intel also announced that it was seeking to cut more jobs and also scale back some projects in Europe.

Investors did not like Intel's results, nor its outlook for Q3, causing shares to skid 5% in the after-hours market. Intel is going to remain a restructuring play in the near term, but I see upside potential if the chipmaker can turn around its core operations and lower its losses in its core chipmaking unit.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Intel a strong buy in April due to the possibility of a turnaround in its core business: 2025 Could Be A Rebound Year. Unfortunately, Intel’s financial situation did not improve much in the last quarter, due to a continual restructuring that is necessitating additional job cuts. However, as the company restructures and streamlines its operations, there is an opportunity for Intel to lower its operating expenses and boost its profitability, especially if the chipmaker succeeds in narrowing its losses in the foundry business.

Short-Term Turnaround Risks Need To Be Balanced Against Long-Term Restructuring Upside

Intel's continual restructuring caused the chipmaker to drastically underperform EPS estimates: in the second quarter, Intel generated a loss of $0.10 per share, missing the consensus prediction by $0.11 per share. The top line was flat, year-over-year, at $12.9B, but beat the revenue consensus forecast by $1.02B.

In total, Intel generated $12.9B in revenue in the second quarter, showing no growth compared to the year-earlier period. The chipmaker's gross margins also dropped further, to 29.7% based off of non-GAAP figures, showing a sequential decline of 9.5 PP. The Client Computing Group, which is the biggest segment for Intel, representing about 61% of consolidated revenue, saw a 3% top line decrease compared to the year-earlier period.

Intel continued to deal with a major restructuring of its core operations in the second quarter that resulted in layoffs as well as other measures, such as the scrapping of projects in Europe and slowed construction for one of its chip plants in Ohio. The chipmaker previously said that it will lay 15% of its global workforce and seeks to bring its employee count down to 75,000 by the end of the current fiscal year. These strategic actions are meant to lower Intel's operating expenses to ~$17.0B annually and streamline operations. Intel plans on further lowering its operating expenses to $16.0B next year.

In the second quarter, Intel recognized $1.9B in restructuring expenses related to the layoff of workers around the world. This restructuring expense led to a negative non-GAAP EPS impact amounting to $0.20/share... which is why Intel missed second quarter earnings estimates.

Another headache for Intel, in addition to pressure on its top line as well as higher-than-expected restructuring expenses, is the foundry business... which continues to lose a lot of money for the chipmaker. In the second quarter, Intel's chip-making unit generated $4.4B in total revenue, showing $100M Y/Y growth, but it is still losing too much money: Intel lost $3.2B (in operating losses) on $4.4B in revenue, highlighting a deeply loss-making business unit.

For Intel to succeed here, the chipmaker either needs to sign a big deal with other chip companies for the production of AI chips, or seek out a strategic (joint venture) solution with one of its rivals, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). TSMC this year announced a massive $200B investment program into the United States, so the Taiwanese foundry is definitely a company that I could see as a strategic partner for Intel going forward.

Intel’s Outlook

The outlook for the third quarter was a disappointment and likely explains why shares slid 5% after the Q2 earnings report card. The company expects breakeven EPS and a gross margin of 36%, which implies a 6 PP margin improvement Q/Q, on a non-GAAP basis. Therefore, with the outlook incorporating the expectation of a sequential margin rebound, I believe that investors responded a bit too negatively, and I continue to see shares of Intel as a strong buy.

Intel’s Valuation

Intel is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 28.8X, which is a high valuation multiplier for a company in a restructuring. However, as the company restructures and focuses on its core operations, such as the Client Computing Group and Data Centers, lowers operating expenses, and rationalizes its CapEx spend, Intel has an opportunity to boost its EPS growth potential. Further, I continue to expect Intel to offer up a strategic solution for its chip-making unit, which could help the company narrow its operating losses.

Compared to chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which are crushing it right now in the GPU and AI accelerator market, Intel is sitting idly on the sidelines and focused on cutting costs, so it is easy to see why Intel's share price is languishing and Nvidia's reached a new all-time high lately.

Nvidia is currently the most valuable company in the world with a market cap exceeding $4 trillion and shares are valued at a P/E ratio, on a forward FY 2026 basis, of 29.8X. I wrote about the potential for accelerating CapEx spending in my work on Nvidia -- Why I Am Accumulating Nvidia At All-Time Highs -- and continue to see the chip-design company as fundamentally undervalued.

AMD is trading at a 28.0X P/E ratio and has a ton of catalysts in its business, most of which relate to growing AI spend and an increasingly filled AI accelerator pipeline: Avalanche Of Catalysts. Additionally, both Nvidia and AMD received a boost from federal regulators which have greenlighted the resumption of chip exports to China.

While Intel's stock seems comparatively expensive, given its restructuring backdrop, Intel has potential to grow its EPS on the back of a rebound in the Client Computing Group, operating expense cuts and, potentially, a joint venture announcement with regard to its foundry operations. In my last work on Intel, I calculated a fair value of $33 per share for the chipmaker based off of historical P/E ratios. I believe this price target will be hard to achieve for Intel in the next twelve months as it continues to work through its restructuring, but it is still a viable long-term stock price target, in my opinion, if the restructuring ultimately is successful.

Risks With Intel

Intel is currently focused on restructuring of its core operations that included the sale of a majority position in Altera, Intel's autonomous vehicle technology business. This transaction is expected to close in the third quarter. If Intel’s restructuring turns out to be successful and the company returns to Client Computing Group-driven top line and earnings growth, I see potential for a stock repricing. However, if Intel fails to reposition its business or lower its operating expenses, shares may languish for a very long time.

Final Thoughts

Intel's second quarter earnings report card was not great, and the outlook for the third quarter was a disappointment as well in terms of EPS. However, the gross margin outlook indicates that the chipmaker expects a rebound here in the short term, which could indicate a stabilization of Intel's business and could help drive a change in sentiment. Further, I fully expect Intel to present investors with a strategic solution to its problems in its chipmaking unit (beyond just mere cost cuts and delayed capital projects) as it loses way too much money on its foundry business in relation to the revenues this business brings in.

In my opinion, the most likely solution, is a potential joint venture agreement with another foundry, like TSMC, in order to share operating and development costs for new AI chips that could be fully manufactured in the U.S. in the future. While Intel's stock is currently out-of-favor, a successful restructuring and a long-term foundry partnership deal with another large-scale chip manufacturer could change investor sentiment and yield attractive long-term returns for investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brad Simpson On Whether The Record Run For Markets Can Continue
data.description:
Markets continue to make record highs, but can that momentum continue with a U.S. trade deadline looming? Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth, discusses how investors are grappling with tariffs, the latest corporate earnings, and ongoing uncertainty.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - We've got markets at record highs, heading into another US trade deadline. We've got a lot of positive momentum in equities, but is it going to be tested? Brad Simpson, chief wealth strategist, TD Wealth, joins us now with more. Brad, great to have you here.

Brad Simpson - Greg, it's great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - You brought some pictures for us and some charts to help tell the story. And as we laid it out, looking at markets, pushing on a daily basis, it feels like now into new highs, we've still got a lot to worry about. Are markets decoupled from the economic reality?

Brad Simpson - That markets might ultimately be decoupled from reality. So, I guess, right off the bat, I know usually we have some pleasantries, and then we go into a chart. But I think this really is a great way to start this. In about a week or so, we're going to be coming to the deadline for tariffs. And of course, that was supposed to be one of the big risks that are out there. And what we're looking at here is the direction of the S&P 500.

The arrow that you see going down, it's actually inverted. So what we're seeing there is trade policy risk and tariff risk is actually headed up. And if some of the concern out there is about tariffs and somehow on that August 1, because we've sort of seen along the way some big drops, this is a market that's not terribly afraid or worried about tariffs at this point. Now, there are some good reasons for that, I think, right out of the gate.

Mansi Desai, who's a senior analyst on my team, published a paper called Made in the USA about, I think, 10 days ago. You can find it on my LinkedIn, and it's a fantastic article, where she breaks down US trade policy but really makes, I think, a really convincing case that the US underestimated the strength of the folks they were going to be negotiating with. And particularly in China, which there's a long way to go to be able to negotiate this. But she makes, I think, a really brilliant point to say that America didn't hold or doesn't hold the cards in the big stick that they thought in these negotiations. And so what we're seeing, as we saw, the agreement of 15% with Japan last night that there's a better tone around trade now. And that better trade, ultimately, is, I think, them coming to the conclusion, yeah, they've got a strong negotiating position, but they didn't have the negotiating position that they thought they did.

And so we, right now and what we've had since our last quarter, was -- said, look, we think it's going to be somewhere between 15% and 20%. That's our 45% base case scenario for tariffs. That's certainly where they're starting to come in. And I think that's -- out of the gate, I think that's what we're looking at.

Now, to the second part of that question and your comments on the way in, is I kind of laughed to myself in your introduction. When part of your introduction is talking about the strength of meme stocks, that's usually, we better get our chief strategist out.

Greg Bonnell - What's going on?

Brad Simpson - What's going on, right? And so I think a little bit is that I want to draw a picture for you and folks who are in today and go, okay, so let's look at this picture and then what conclusions would you come to from it? So we start out of the gate right now, S&P 500. We know that there's a V-shaped recovery that we've seen, a rally that we've seen, again, has been focused on big-name and tech and tech companies, and now sitting at highs that we have -- highs that we've seen of concentration of these big-name tech stocks of roughly around 33%, 34% in the S&P 500. So let's call that we've eclipsed the peak of what we saw for concentration back in 1999.

And of course, we know a little bit what happened from there. But if you look at the size of this, let's call it somewhere between $18 trillion and $20 trillion in market cap. That means one subsector of the S&P 500 now is larger than any other nation's index. Just stop and let that soak in a little bit. So there's point one. Let's set that in a little bit, right? There you go, okay, when we're publishing our portfolio strategy quarterly for next quarter -- or for next quarter, coming up next week, and it's called Still Here. And why did we call it Still Here?

It's this idea of a year and a half ago, we introduced this concept of gray rhino. And a gray rhino, of course, is risks that we know that are there, but you can see them coming, but you do nothing about. So one we made the argument was this idea of deglobalization. And this is something -- and this is a little bit what we're managing through right now. The other one was AI. What is this going to mean? The positives and the negatives to them.

And this movement we're seeing is, if you want to look at what's making tech things move, it always comes down to, well, it's AI. Well, it's AI. Well, it's AI. And you've got to be careful with monocausal reasons when you underline those. The third one we underlined is war. And of course, we've got three pretty actively engaged theaters of war going on right now. Now, interestingly, these risks, the market has said, well, they're off to the side right now. And I think they are cooler than what they were. But we got to remember, they're kind of out there still, right? So we look at those and you go, the VIX is at 16 right now, long-dated puts, okay? If I wanted to go and I wanted to buy insurance on the protection of my portfolio, are dirt cheap right now.

So which means that the market is saying that I don't need to worry about it. I don't need to think of--

Greg Bonnell - Are spreads another part of that story? I know you have a picture about spreads right now being historic. So you're talking about relatively risk-free debt, compared to...

Brad Simpson - Yeah, so let's look at -- I brought a chart along for spreads. Let's look at this. So what we're looking at here is, so this is in fixed-income markets. And this is looking at the spreads between corporates from quality corporates over to the high-yield stuff and looking at it over treasuries.

Now, the one bang in the middle there is that the spread of corps over a 10-year Treasury is at the 0 percentile. So you see every one of those little black diamonds there, all at the bottom of every one of those bars? What that says is that, when you're looking at the spreads for corporate debt right now, from quality to junk, is at the absolute bottom, meaning it's the fixed -- it's the non-government side of the fixed-income market saying there's no risk. So we check that one off.

Okay, I brought another slide. And let's take a look at this. This one we're looking at factor exposure. So often, when we're talking about when you're allocating a portfolio, asset allocation isn't enough. You also want to think about factor exposure, factors like the DNA of what makes an equity company.

So if we can put up the factor chart I brought and take a look at this, what we can see is that -- no, not that one.

Greg Bonnell - Growth and higher risk, growth...

Brad Simpson - No. Nope. We're going to come to that one. That's the -- there we go. So let's look at this. This is looking at first half of 2024 performance of equity markets, but through a factor lens. So the DNA of what makes the equity market roll, what's driving it?

Well, momentum up 12%. Growth up 13%. There rest, low volatility, value, small cap, all in the negative. So the one fundamental -- it's like inside of your body, if you had one driver making yourself go, that's the one that's making it go. Okay, let's look at the next one. It's the last one. And again, the idea here is that, let's step back and just look at the world through a different lens and just think about this a little. So what we're looking at here is the S&P 500 move, which is the black line. The Renaissance IPO Index. So what were them there is, it's just the most speculative part of the market that you want to consider. And money is plowing into there. The top green one there is the SPAC Index. The SPAC Index is just the ability to go out and accumulate flow that are going to go into SPACs. How many people have been talking SPACs here on this show over the last couple of years?

Greg Bonnell - We've not been talking SPACs very much at all.

Brad Simpson - Right? You don't even hear SPACs. So these were absolutely dead, left at the side of the road. And where we are looking at today is, all of a sudden, this is back in vogue again. And my point with all of this is you go one way or the other and that -- and again, to bring it up, we're also talking about meme stocks once again. There's lots of money in the system. There's really, really, really -- it's really, really quiet with folks at work moving assets right now. In the equity market, if you're moving into the second half, what we saw in the first half -- and we wrote a little bit about this -- is that once we went after Liberation Day, retail or wealth clients really started to move into the back end of these tech names, and managers who've been underperforming the benchmark for the last couple of years, start looking down and say, wait a minute, I might underperform again this year as well. So they've all started to move into this.

So the last one in my picture of this is when the Joes and the Pros both are plowing into something, and these are all the steps, I'm not trying to lead us to an alarming ending here, but what I do want to do is a little bit is to say that let's think about this a little bit.

And if you had a chance to say, for my speculative money, this is what this market is. For my money that I'm investing based on my wealth plan, where I want to grow and I want to protect, and I'm thinking about funding things in my liability and things that down the road I want to fund, I think this is a place where you want to stop a little bit and make sure that there's a strong barrier between these two, that says how I'm going to allocate over here, where I'm going to speculate here.

But know that when we turn the tap back on a little bit -- and we can talk about one of the things that I think might be the thing that cracked this thing a little -- that we don't get caught up in this. And I think we're seeing some of that right now.

Greg Bonnell - Okay, we got about a minute. So you just teased people with what could crack it. Give us a little taste.

Brad Simpson - Well, so I mean, I think the long story short is that, in our publication next week, what we do is we do a deep dive into the US labor market. And why is that important? We know that ultimately the US economy is driven by the consumer. The consumer feels good when they have their job.

When we look at the labor market, it's very, very good in the United States. However, we have seen the last nonfarm payrolls come out, and they were starting to turn over. And when we're seeing adjustments to those nonfarm payrolls, we see them adjusted and we see them headed downwards.

When we take the consumer part of that labor part and look at the health of the consumer, what we can see in active data now, what we can see is both in restaurants and entertainment, we start to see those starting to roll over. When we split the high-income earners, low-income earners, low-income earners threw in the towel about 18 months ago. High-income earners are starting to really slow as well, I think.

And so what we want to go is to say that -- you and I have talked a lot about labor and consumer on these before, but I think in this one for us is that it's not that they're saying the end is nigh, but there is something in this labor market that is telling us that the growth in the United States is slowing. And it's not an inevitable recession, but we think that these are one of the things that could provide a crack as we move when markets turn back up again, when volumes turn back on and we enter into September.

Original Post

data.title: Alphabet Q2: Decimates The Bear Case, Likely The Cheapest Mag 7 Stock
data.description:
I got asked a question on a podcast earlier in the week about Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Tesla's (TSLA) Q2 earnings. I had said that I expected GOOGL to rally and TSLA to decline, as it would be very difficult for TSLA to produce strong EPS metrics. GOOGL didn't disappoint, and I am shocked that its share price didn't rally more. GOOGL delivered $96.43 billion in revenue, which was a 13.8% YoY increase and a beat of $2.45 billion on the consensus estimates. GOOGL also generated $2.31 in GAAP EPS, which came in ahead of the consensus estimates by $0.12. The biggest headwind for GOOGL has been Search, as the bear case has been built around Search being cannibalized by ChatGPT and other LLMs, which would negatively impact their revenue. This story has been playing out for several quarters now, and enough is enough. The amount of revenue generated by Google Search & other came in at $54.19 billion, which was an increase of 11.71% YoY and 6.88% QoQ. It's time that the market accepts the fact that GOOGL has built a phenomenal business that continues to grow, and it's still the cheapest component of the Magnificent Seven on a valuation basis. The headwinds from a sentiment perspective may not be alleviated, but I strongly feel that GOOGL is worth over $250 per share and that investors are getting a great deal at the current levels. There is nothing not to like; GOOGL is still growing; it's buying back over $10 billion worth of shares per quarter; it's now paying a dividend; there is more than $95 billion on the balance sheet as cash; and they're able to allocate as much capital as needed to implement their growth plans.

Following Up on My Previous Article About GOOGL

I previously wrote an article about GOOGL at the end of April (can be read here) where I discussed how their Q1 results should push the stock higher. Since then, shares of GOOGL have increased by 16.42%, while the S&P 500 has climbed 15.91%. Google's Q1 2025 earnings beat expectations with $90.23 billion in revenue and $2.81 GAAP EPS as they delivered strong growth in Search and Google Cloud. I felt that the valuation was too cheap and that the narrative about Search being cannibalized was overblown. I am following up with a new article about why I am more bullish than before about GOOGL, as they just showed the market how powerful their assets really are. I think that GOOGL should be a minimum of $250; if they get there anytime soon is a different story.

Risks to Investing in GOOGL

While I am more bullish than ever about GOOGL, there are several risk factors to consider. The bears could be correct that Google Search will be negatively impacted in the future. We do not know what type of technology companies are working on, and Mark Zuckerberg has previously discussed a post-smartphone world. In the future, there could be new technologies that replace many functions from Google Search. We are also experiencing a growth spurt in cloud computing, and at some point, that will end. Google Cloud may have an inflection point in the worst way after a 31.67% YoY growth rate. If growth in the cloud slows or if AWS and Azure figure out a way to impede Google Cloud's growth, it could hurt the investment thesis for GOOGL. There is also the threat of antitrust lawsuits and the potential for GOOGL to be broken up, as many look at them as a monopoly. If that occurs, GOOGL wouldn't have the economies of scale it benefits from, and its investment case could be impacted. Investors should do their due diligence because investing in GOOGL comes with several risk factors that can't be ignored.

GOOGL Decimated the Bear Case in Q2, and There Was Nothing to Dislike

GOOGL threw the kitchen sink at the market this quarter, and I firmly believe it decimated the bear case. Google generated $96.43 billion in revenue, which was a 13.79% increase YoY and a 6.86% increase QoQ. GOOGL generated $1.06 billion of revenue per day in Q2. On a profitability level, GOOGL produced $28.2 billion in net income, which is $309.85 million per day, and their EPS increased by 22.22% YoY from $1.89 to $2.31. This was largely due to Google Search and Google Cloud. GOOGL must not have gotten the memo about search being dead because Google Search generated $54.19 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 11.71% YoY. Google Cloud delivered an incredible increase of 31.67% YoY and 11.13% QoQ as it generated $13.62 billion in revenue during Q2. Google Cloud's annual revenue run-rate now exceeds $50 billion, and its operating income more than doubled to $2.8 billion. The growth is far from over, as Google Cloud's backlog increased 38% YoY to $106 billion, which signals there is extreme demand from its customers. These numbers are defying what most investors thought would be possible, and just these 2 segments alone are producing $67.81 billion in revenue with a run rate of $271.26 billion annually.

I feel as if the rest of GOOGL's businesses don't get enough attention, as the spotlight has been on Google Search and Google Cloud. YouTube ads generated $9.8 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 13.08% YoY and 9.73% QoQ. YouTube Shorts is now earning as much revenue per watch hour as traditional in-stream videos in the U.S. and exceeding traditional video in some countries as they are now capturing 200 billion daily views globally. What I found the most interesting is that on the earnings call they discussed how the Nielsen data confirmed that YouTube has led U.S. streaming watch time over the past 2 years, and it reached a record of 12.8% of total TV viewing time in June of 2025. The other bright spot is Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices, as it grew 20.31% YoY and 7.94% QoQ to $11.2 billion. Keep in mind that YouTube subscriptions are part of this, and we are seeing a pattern where revenue drops off in Q1 then gains sequentially through Q4. There is nothing in the numbers to make me think that this will slow down. Aspects of the business, such as Waymo, aren't even contributing to the valuation, and in the next 5-10 years, that could be a huge standalone business on its own. GOOGL's business is firing on all cylinders, and nothing in its metrics supports the bear narrative.

In Q2 GOOGL's operating income increased 14% to $31.3 billion, which is an operating margin of 32.4%. I don't understand how anyone can formulate a bear case when a company has an operating margin this high with a run rate that exceeds $120 billion in operating income. GOOGL is well-positioned for continued growth, which is driven by its differentiated full-stack approach to artificial intelligence (AI). GOOGL is a leader in AI research with cutting-edge models and product and platform integrations that bring AI to end users globally. GOOGL's investments in AI infrastructure are crucial, as they include its global network of AI-optimized data centers, storage innovations, and a wide range of TPUs and GPUs. GOOGL is accelerating its infrastructure investments, and they are expecting to allocate approximately $85 billion toward CapEx in 2025. AI is impacting GOOGL's segments across the board, starting with Google Search, as AI Overviews has over 2 billion monthly users and AI Mode has over 100 million monthly active users. GOOGL has seen increased query volume and user engagement, which signals search is not being cannibalized. The Gemini app has grown to over 450 million monthly active users and it's seeing an increase of over 50% QoQ in daily requests. It's going to be difficult for the competition to compete with GOOGL considering they can take a blank check approach toward innovation. The main problem that I have with the bear case is that people are writing GOOGL off as if they're not going to deploy capital to fortify their moat. Companies don't get to GOOGL's level without being at the top of their game in every aspect, and the metrics indicate that GOOGL is going to deploy as much capital as needed to continue providing the best products it can and continue its forward growth momentum.

GOOGL Is Still the Most Undervalued Magnificent Seven Stock

GOOGL's valuation is still painful to look at, as the market isn't giving GOOGL much respect. GOOGL is now expected to produce $9.95 of EPS in 2025 and $11.86 of EPS in 2027. GOOGL is trading at 19.31 times 2025 earnings and is expected to grow its EPS by 19.2% over the next 2 years. Apple (AAPL) has roughly the same amount of forward EPS growth on the horizon and is trading at 29.69 times 2025 earnings. When I look out to 2027, GOOGL is trading at 16.20 times 2027 earnings, which is an extreme value in my opinion. It's the only Magnificent Seven company that trades at less than 20 times 2027 earnings. To put things in perspective, old-world conglomerates such as The Coca-Cola Company (KO) trade at 20.08 times 2027 earnings and The Procter & Gamble Company trades at 21.25 times 2027 earnings. GOOGL still looks very cheap based on the amount of profitability they generate.

When I look at how much cash from operations GOOGL is producing, it's crazy to think more investors aren't bullish on them. In the trailing twelve months (TTM) GOOGL has generated $133.71 billion in cash from operations, which is the most in the Magnificent Seven. Their CapEx spend has increased, but they have still produced $66.73 billion in free cash flow (FCF). GOOGL is trading at 35.05 times its FCF, which is the 3rd cheapest in the group, but once they are done with their CapEx buildout, they should be able to go into maintenance mode and significantly increase their FCF overnight. GOOGL is in a position where it can continue growing its profitability, and every dollar of new revenue has a 36% cash from operations yield. Investors can get a huge stream of cash flow for an inexpensive price at these levels.

Conclusion

I believe the market is incorrect and GOOGL is worth at least $250 per share. I have said $250 because when I change GOOGL's share price to $250, it would still be trading at 25.13 times 2025 earnings and 21.08 times 2027 earnings based on the current analyst projections, which are still the lowest valuations in the Magnificent Seven. Since GOOGL has the lowest projected earnings growth, I feel it's fair to keep the valuation under Meta Platforms (META) and the rest of its peer group. I think there is a lot of room for shares of GOOGL to move higher as they continue to dismantle the bear case around Google Search's ability to generate top-line revenue growth. GOOGL had a phenomenal quarter as its total revenue increased by 13.79% YoY, led by Google Search and Google Cloud. The fact that Google Clouds backlog increased 38% YoY to $106 billion signals how much demand there still is and that the growth story isn't going to fade away in the cloud anytime soon. I think investors are underestimating GOOGL's relevance in AI and their ability to protect their underlying businesses. I think we're going to look back on this period and see how undervalued GOOGL truly was in several years from now.

data.title: The U.S. Economy Is Slowing, But Expect Growth To Surprise To The Upside
data.description:
As it stands, hard economic data in the US has shown signs of weakness over the past couple of quarters. But, contrary to the belief of many, the US economy remains robust and far from recessionary levels.

For the most part, key economic data is at or above average levels. Industrial production - the most cyclical of the primary hard data points, has experienced some negative growth since 2022 and has been a minor detractor from the economy, but has also improved modestly over the past 12 months and has not detracted anywhere near levels akin to past recessions. Meanwhile, employment growth has slowed and remains at average levels. The same can be said of consumption, manufacturing and trade sales, disposable income and retail sales.

Though we have seen modest weakness, growth in these key economic data points remains positive and robust on the whole.

Confirming this is the Conference Board’s Coincident Economic Index, which has been suggesting the US economy has been growing consistently in the 2-3% range since 2022, a level slightly above its long-term average.

We can perhaps view this dynamic more clearly when comparing these key hard data points to their respective historical time series. Only consumption and personal income growth are showing notable signs of weakness. The cyclical data points that tend to drive the business cycle in industrial production, manufacturing and trade sales as well as retail sales remain at or above average levels seen over the past 50 years.

Again, we have seen some stress in economic data, but it is nothing particularly troubling as yet. Growth is slowing, but still to a lesser extent than consensus expects it to.

Focusing on some of the more cyclical hard economic data, namely the manufacturing and construction sectors, we are again not seeing any clear signs of recessionary activity in the US. Manufacturing production and durable goods new orders have both accelerated in 2025, while business investment also continues to grow persistently at positive levels. Construction spending is one area of the US economy currently experiencing a modest contraction, while cyclical employment growth has also been relatively weak, though it remains positive.

For the US to enter a recession, we would likely need to see all these cyclical data points exhibiting negative growth (which would flow through to weakness in industrial production and retail trade), which is clearly not the case at present.

On the soft data side, there has been notably more weakness, thanks largely to the uncertainty surrounding tariff policies and inflation. Both the manufacturing and non-manufacturing ISM PMIs have been in or close to the sub-50 contractionary territory in 2025.

The same can largely be said of consumer and small business confidence surveys, which have been relatively weak this year, again thanks largely to tariff policies and the uncertainty surrounding Trump 2.0.

The fact that these soft data points have yet to translate into material hard data weakness illustrates just how robust the US economy has been. It is also important to remember that the correlation between confidence surveys and actual underlying economic activity is spurious. It is true that we are likely to see soft data uncertainty show up in weaker hard data to some degree, but leading economic indicators will provide a clearer picture of the extent to which this ought to occur than coincidental soft data.

What we can be sure of is the extent to which fiscal largesse has been a key driver of the US economy. Government transfer payments continue to grow at around 10% p.a., and with Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill on the horizon and DOGE in the dustbin, this dynamic is unlikely to change anytime soon.

The US economy is likely to continue to prove robust so long as the government is running a ~6% budget deficit. Simple as that.

It is also worth remembering how US households, on average, continue to find themselves in as good a financial position as they have been in a generation. Household debt levels and debt serviceability are both at multi-decade lows, while household net worth and cash balances are at levels well above their long-term trends. These are perhaps the most important charts to consider when viewing the current state of the US economy. Growth will slow, but it is very unlikely for the US to experience a recession when households are in the position they currently find themselves, particularly when we consider that consumption is roughly 70% of the US economy.

Leading indicators show a mixed outlook, but aren’t pointing to recession

As we have seen, hard economic data on a coincident basis remains solid though not outstanding. The Atlanta Fed’s GDP Nowcast is currently pointing to 2.4% real GDP growth through Q2. That seems about right given what we have reviewed above, and is slightly higher than Q1 real GDP of 1.9%, as well as consensus expectations for Q2, which are around 2%. Nominal GDP growth of around 5% is a solid state of affairs for risk assets.

It again appears likely that economic growth surprised to the upside during Q2. This is a dynamic I have talked about in the past, with the significantly weaker dollar we saw during Q2, a big tailwind to upside economic surprises. There remains little reason to think this will change materially over the next few months.

The Fed’s Weekly Economic Index is certainly not suggesting that any notable breakdown in hard economic data is coming through July.

On a leading indicator front, we continue to see a modestly slower outlook for the US economy, but not materially slower.

The Conference Board’s LEI has been largely trending higher over the past 24 months, but remains negative and could be in the process of rolling over. However, the relationship between this indicator and coincident hard data has deteriorated post-COVID.

My own composite LEI for the US economy is pointing to sideways growth over the next six months.

Lead indicators of the volatile manufacturing sector continue to show a mixed outlook.

While the ISM New Order less Inventories spread has likely bottomed, it is not yet suggesting that a big rise in the manufacturing PMI is imminent.

Meanwhile, lead indicators of goods inventories are currently suggesting a small rise in inventories is imminent, which would be a boon to growth so long as sales follow suit.

On the other hand, manufacturing backlogs via the ISM survey are not pointing to a material rise in manufacturing inventories, though backlogs are not suggesting an imminent decline in inventories.

As such, the outlook for the manufacturing sector and hard data, such as industrial production, remains mixed.

Non-residential fixed investment is one area that could continue to detract from growth over the next few months, given the recent decline in the Regional Fed capex intentions surveys. However, it is important to remember that capex intentions surveys are a volatile figure, so I would need to see a few more months of poor data in this series to be more concerned about a continued contraction in business capex.

A dynamic that is still supportive of economic growth is the wealth effect. As we can see below, growth in household net worth as a percent of GDP has seen significant increases over the past couple of years. And although this indicator has likely reached a cyclical high, it remains supportive of GDP growth through Q2 and Q3.

Financial conditions overall continue to support the business cycle. We can see below the relationship between the Federal Reserve’s Financial Conditions Index and the ISM Manufacturing PMI.

The biggest drivers of this easing in financial conditions have been collapsing credit spreads and rising stock prices. There is a clear reflexivity between the stock market and the US economy, and as it currently stands, a strong stock market is supportive of economic growth and vice versa.

My Macro Conditions Index, which consists of only the dollar, commodity prices, and interest rates, and tends to lead the ISM Manufacturing PMI by around nine months, also continues to trend notably higher.

However, one area of concern has been the recent decline in CEO Confidence, an indicator that is generally highly correlated to stock prices. This data has yet to be released for Q2, and given the wind back of tariffs and stock market rebound we have seen in Q2, I am expecting his index to rebound notably. If it does not, then it appears likely we will see this translate into continued weakness in business investment and potential layoffs.

But for now, we should expect US households to continue to consume at a reasonable pace. My Consumer Spending lead indicator has recently moved notably higher, which is supportive of consumption and retail sales.

What’s more, bank lending standards for credit cards continue to ease modestly. Again, this is supportive of household consumption on the margins.

On the employment front, my Leading Employment Index remains in a modest uptrend and is suggesting we are unlikely to see any kind of material decline in employment and economic growth over the next couple of quarters.

Without a material decline in employment growth and a rise in the unemployment rate, the US economy will remain robust, even if it is weaker on net.

Another key driver of the business cycle is the US housing market, given its link to total investment and construction spending. As it stands, housing lead indicators such as growth in building permits and the NAHB housing index are largely trending sideways. Likewise, mortgage rates (which themselves lead the aforementioned data, as we can see below), are also largely trending sideways. As such, we should expect to see construction spending continue to trend in a sideways fashion over the coming quarters.

Residential investment is another housing-related indicator that suggests sideways to slightly slower economic growth in the US remains the most likely outcome over the next six to nine months.

Overall, economic lead indicators are suggesting US economic growth will slow on the margins, but is unlikely to see any material weakness anytime soon.

The global picture

On a global basis, we are seeing some improvement in lead indicators outside of the US. The story for the past 18 months has been significant US economic (and thus stock market) outperformance versus the rest of the world. We are now slowly seeing signs of a reversal of this dynamic.

As we can see below, lead indicators for China and Europe have shown signs of improvement in 2025 and are starting to catch up to US LEIs.

This is particularly the case in China, which is a positive outcome for the global economy, though we are not out of the woods yet.

Inflation pressures remain, but are fully expected by the market

On the inflation front, the story remains the same as it has been for most of 2025: inflation will accelerate through the second half of 2025, but it will largely be on the goods side as opposed to the services side.

We can see below how the majority of inflation lead indicators are accelerating, with the exception being wage growth, rent, and shelter.

Unless services inflation leads indicators start to turn high in the next few months, this will help put a ceiling on how much inflation will actually rise this year. I expect we will see 3.5-4% inflation on a headline basis, but I am not yet convinced it will be a sustained inflation spike.

Nonetheless, this is a headwind to dovish monetary policy and probably a headwind to risk assets on the margins. However, it is worth noting that markets are fully expecting inflation to rise through the end of 2025, so how much of a headwind it is for equities remains to be seen. Obviously, higher inflation is not positive for bonds.

Markets priced in a lot of weakness in April

If we turn now to how risk assets are priced relative to the business cycle, it is worth remembering that the S&P 500 did a solid job of pricing in a low 40s PMI during the April sell-off. While the market didn’t price in a recession, it has done a solid job of pricing in a slowdown, meaning that even if growth slows on the margins as I expect it to, that is not really a notable headwind for equities, given what they are pricing in.

We can see below how my cyclical Fair Value Model for the S&P 500 is closer to undervalued than overvalued.

The April sell-off saw the biggest unwind in investor sentiment and positioning since 2022. Markets and investors fully priced in a notable growth slowdown in April, hence why we have seen markets grind higher since. Growth has been holding up better than expected, and as a result, underweight managers have been forced to chase the market higher.

As it currently stands, investor positioning is now much less of a tailwind than it was in April. Though a number of positioning indicators are still relatively light, such as S&P 500 net speculative positioning, quite a few are closing in on levels that tend to coincide with market tops.

Summary, outlook, and key macro notes:

  • Due to historically strong household and corporate balance sheets, persistent fiscal support, lax immigration, and labor hoarding, the US economy has continued to be resilient over the past two years, despite a multi-decade high in interest rates. Trump 2.0’s tariff and DOGE policies do not yet appear to be bringing down the US economy, and look increasingly unlikely to do so as Trump pivots away from trade wars to more populist-type policies.

  • Households and corporations have been able to weather the storm of rising interest rates, leading to an income-driven business cycle. It is difficult for the US to enter a recession, given the excellent state of household balance sheets, while strong corporate balance sheets are also supportive of the labor market. However, the best of the resilient US economy is now behind us.

  • Overall, the US economy continues to grow at an average pace, with hard data far from recessionary levels.

  • Economic lead indicators continue to hold up, and are not signaling any notable rise in unemployment or economic weakness forthcoming. However, US economic growth is likely to slow on the margins, though it will probably continue to surprise to the upside.

  • Trump wants a US economy that is not reliant on government spending and is instead driven by the private sector. However, thanks to the bond market turmoil, mid-term election pressures, and the checks and balances of the US political system, Trump appears to be pivoting away from his pro-tariff and deficit reduction policies and back toward more populist policies that got him elected in the first place. This likely means the negative impacts from tariffs and DOGE are likely to be much smaller than expected, while the “Big Ugly Bill” is likely to continue the fiscal impulse into 2026 and 2027, a supportive backdrop for the US economy.

  • As a result, the US economy and labor market should continue to hold together better than expected.

Biggest risks facing markets in 2025:

  • The Fed is unable to sufficiently ease monetary policy thanks to the above target and accelerating inflation. However, it is important to remember that historically, fiscal dominance has generally led to a Federal Reserve that is supportive of the Treasury by having a dovish reaction function despite data to the contrary. Trump replacing Powell with a “yes man” as the new Fed chair may well be a right tail risk not fully anticipated by markets.

  • The repatriation of global capital out of US equity and bond markets back to domestic markets. The rise in European fiscal spending and the monetary policy nominalization in Japan are both legitimate dynamics that represent falling demand for US Treasuries and risk assets.

  • The forthcoming rise in inflation could upset markets later in 2025. However, this dynamic is now fully anticipated by markets, so it may not be the risk it once was.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: A Tale Of 2 Railways
data.description:
Some weeks ago, I wrote an article about Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.A), (NYSE:BRK.B) and their Precision Castparts (PCC) acquisition, which was partially written off as a mistake in 2020, but is doing much better now, as demand for turbines is lifted by power-hungry data centers and growing aircraft production rates.

Some readers commented asking for additional material on other pieces of Berkshire, and here is my response. Any further ideas, please let me know.

I’ve chosen BNSF as the next topic to dive into. Although the outcome is decidedly more favorable for Berkshire, there is room for improvement at the railroad as well. What follows is a review of BNSF and their performance on a head-to-head comparison with their main competitor, Union Pacific Corporation (UNP).

Some context on Berkshire and the BNSF acquisition:

I have a “Strong Buy” rating on Berkshire, which needs some context around it. Under normal circumstances, Berkshire will struggle to outperform the market. Returns will be dragged down by the +$300B in t-bills that they hold beyond the amount retained to cushion the insurance business. Earning barely 4%, these will drag down overall returns.

Experienced Berkshire owners recognize the t-bills as a mountain of dry powder for when “adverse circumstances” prevail. Paraphrasing Warren, it is certain that people will periodically panic, but it is impossible to know when.

Thus, I strongly believe that a significant position on BRK will help almost any portfolio, precisely because it is bound to do better during and after the storm. In other words, the “worst case” for Berkshire is perpetual blue skies, yielding little chance to deploy capital either in distressed securities or Berkshire’s own shares. Not bad for the rest of the portfolio, but Berkshire's cash hoard will continue to pile on. If you are certain we’ve seen the last crash, then don’t hold Berkshire.

Anyway, with that out of the way…using rough numbers, Berkshire is valued at $1T currently by the market. With t-bills worth par, we can deduce that “all the rest” is worth around $700B (again, very roughly). These non-cash assets include everything, the insurance operations, the stock positions, the MSR segment and the industrial giants Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE) and BNSF.

BNSF was taken private by Berkshire in early 2010. Buffett purchased the 77.5% of shares he did not already own for $26.5B, implying a valuation (for all the equity) of about $34B. Union Pacific was worth about $38B that same year. Both companies had almost identical revenues of $16.9B that year.

Since then, both companies have grown revenues and profits, but their trajectories have not been the same. UNP is now worth $140B, and we can safely estimate the standalone value for BNSF at some discount to such an amount. Christopher Bloomstran (founder of Semper Augustus and long-time Berkshire investor) estimates $120B-$140B, so let’s go with the low number of $120B.

BNSF would therefore be worth about 17% of the “operating assets” at Berkshire (its share of the $700B), or 12% of the whole thing. Berkshire is so large that even when discussing their massive railroad, we need to remind ourselves that it is still just a part of a much larger conglomerate.

Finally, before we move on to measure the country’s two largest railroads against each other, there is a wider topic to keep in mind. Buffett’s incoming successor, Greg Abel is (at heart) an operator of industrial assets. Not a stranger to M&A by any means, but hailed by Buffett himself as a top-notch business operator. His skillset was sharpened through the build-up of BHE and should transfer well to other regulated and capital-intensive industries. There is no better case study for that than BNSF.

BNSF and UNP since 2010:

The 14-year period looks back to the moment BNSF was taken over by Berkshire Hathaway. Back then, revenues were essentially equal, just under $17B for both, while UNP earned a higher pre-tax profit of $4.4B vs. $4.0B for BNSF.

On the revenue side, we can see a very close correlation between the two. A couple of down-cycles in 2015-16 and 2020, both coinciding with a sharp decline in fuel prices. Although UNP has had higher revenues in the last two years, BNSF surpassed their rival in the five years leading up to 2022. The difference over the whole period is almost nil: UNP averaged $21.7B in revenue per year to BNSF’s $21.8B.

Profits, however, are a different story. Starting slightly ahead, UNP has consistently increased the profitability gap to BNSF. By 2024, their pre-tax earnings reached $8.8B, while BNSF achieved $6.6B.

Since both are working off practically the same revenue base, the margin gap has to come from a different expense base. Capital structure is not a contributor, and if it were, it would help BNSF. Berkshire, even though it could, does not guarantee debt issued at BNSF (or BHE for that matter). Therefore, the capital structure and interest expenses are similar amongst the two railways.

The same goes for depreciation. With similar asset composition, yearly depreciation expense at BNSF has increased from $1.8B when acquired to $2.6B most recently. For UNP, depreciation charges increased from $1.5B to $2.4B. Although contributing somewhat to the profitability gap, the difference is not very material.

The main expense for all railroads is not depreciation, interest or even fuel, but people. Let's look at both fuel and payroll.

Fuel expenses seem “structurally higher" at BNSF, consistently some 4% points higher than UNP (see first chart below). That may be due to different network configurations or the reluctance by BNSF to more fully adopt “precision railroading” methods into their operations.

From the above, both railroads have trended towards better fuel efficiency. From around 20% of revenue at the beginning to less than 15% for BNSF and 10% for UNP. Measured in dollars, the average fuel bill has been $800 million lower for UNP for each of the past three years. This partly explains the $2.2 billion gap in pretax profits, but the full picture needs to include labor.

On payroll expenses, BNSF went backward, from 22% of revenues to 25%, while UNP improved from 24% to 20%. Both have unionized labor, but something happened around 2018 at BNSF. Digging a bit deeper, the difference is explained by headcount, not average pay. Average pay (total compensation and benefits expense divided by total headcount) is $161k for both companies in 2024.

The difference is in the headcount:

I believe 2018 had union negotiations and coincided with a sharp pickup in demand (and a shortage of labor). Some of these factors may have caused BNSF to increase headcount in 2018 while its main rival did not, and then reduce headcount, but never reach the 30k that UNP was able to reach.

So, there we have it. Lower fuel efficiency and 20% higher headcount go a long way toward explaining the performance gap. From very far away, these should be fixable problems. Revenue and under-investment gaps are much harder to fix in capital-intensive businesses, but that is not what is happening here…Easy for me to say, and best of luck Mr. Abel and team.

Not to beat a dead horse, but for completeness I include the operating ratio (sum of all opex divided by revenue), which is widely used as an industry benchmark. Both it and the pre-tax margin show a widening gap in the most recent periods:

On Berkshire Valuation:

Since the early-May announcement, when the stock was at all-time highs, prices have dropped somewhat:

But the stock is still not cheap. I don't expect significant buybacks above $440 (which is some 10% higher than when buybacks stopped in Q2 of 2024).

At the same time, the stock is not very expensive either, especially considering P/E multiples at the broader indices (Berkshire's P/E is lower than it seems, one needs to estimate the proportion of retained earnings from minority stock positions, but this is less relevant as the portfolio shrinks in relation to the total).

Summing up valuation, judging by the company's own repurchase activity (or lack thereof) the stock is trading near intrinsic value. It is, however, a lot less "risky" than the average stock. Courtesy of 1/3 of its value comes from the very safest security one can own. Massive liquidity brings priceless optionality in times of distress; whoever is running it, great opportunities will eventually arrive.

Risk of Owning Berkshire:

As noted, Berkshire has put themselves in a position where the worst case is a below-average return. In any medium to long-term period it is be very difficult to imagine Berkshire losing money for investors. Berkshire is counting on some future accident happening not too far into the future. That is the "risk" in my view, that we go on for three, five or more years without a durable downturn.

If we do see a recession or recession scare strong enough to cause a prolonged market downdraft, then Berkshire will very likely outperform during and after such an event. As is always the case, the best purchases are made by the most patient, and so we wait.

Berkshire therefore offers no risk of "zero", some chance of underperforming the indices, and also some chance of overperformance with the condition precedent of a market crash not too far from now. The relative probabilities of over/under depend on each investor's view on when (not whether) we will next see blood on the streets.

Final thoughts:

Although BNSF is only a part of Berkshire, and likely contributes less than 20% of the overall value, it may serve as an interesting "case study" in light of the ongoing CEO transition. Greg Abel has earned a strong reputation for elevating business operations and BNSF is the largest such opportunity available to him.

The seldom-used elephant gun is hitting 0.66. Overall BNSF has been a decent investment for Berkshire; the initial +$30B is now worth some 4x as much (implying +10% rate of return in addition to distributions for Berkshire). Better than PCC (which was also a +$30B acquisition) but not as good as the $36B Apple stock purchase in 2016.

As for Berkshire as an investment, my view is simple: it adds value in all portfolios; if you already own it as a top position, then hold on to it; if you don't, then consider its huge t-bill stash as dry powder waiting for the next crash, whenever that may be. It will do better than cash, and it will benefit from any future "accident". In other words, it has similar optionality as holding cash directly, but with a lower cost.

data.title: Altisource Portfolio Solutions S.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Altisource Portfolio Solutions S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: BankUnited: Raising The EPS Estimate And Upgrading To Hold
data.description:
I gave BankUnited, Inc. (NYSE:BKU) a sell rating in December 2024. As expected, the stock price has dipped by over 8% since my report. However, the earnings reported have beaten my expectations, as the efficiency ratio has been better than expected. After considering the recent developments, I'm raising my EPS estimate to $3.37 for 2025 from my previous estimate of $3.00. As I'll discuss in greater detail below, I'm expecting growth of securities and the net interest margin to drive earnings this year.

Loan Downtrend Likely to Continue

BankUnited's loan book declined for a fourth consecutive quarter in 2Q'25. According to the details given in the earnings release and the presentation, the period-end loan balance was impacted by the scheduled runoff of residential loans and the unscheduled runoff of commercial & industrial ("C&I") loans. The C&I balance also declined because of strategic exits from some C&I accounts.

The loan balance is likely to continue to face pressure for the remainder of this year because of scheduled runoff in the residential portfolio. The management confirmed in the 2Q'25 conference call that residential runoffs were continuing as previously predicted. In the 1Q'25 conference call, the management had predicted that the residential runoffs would continue for the remainder of the year, and they would revisit this strategy (of letting loans run off) next year.

Moreover, permits for future construction of single-family homes dropped to more than a two-year low in June, according to recent news reports. Due to the demand slump, new housing inventory is at levels last seen in late 2007. I think the only factor that can save this loan segment is if the long end of the yield curve declines, taking mortgage rates down. However, this is unlikely because of upcoming tariff-driven inflation pressures.

My outlook for C&I and commercial real estate ("CRE") is barely better. BankUnited mostly operates in Florida and the New York Tri-State area. It also operates in Dallas, TX, Atlanta, GA, Morristown, NJ, and Charlotte, NC. As shown below, Florida's unemployment rate has inched upwards this year, which signals that business activity in the state is worsening. Further, although New York City's unemployment rate has trended downwards this year, the average is still higher than in 2023.

Moreover, many economists agree that the country's economy is headed for a significant slowdown this year. The Federal Reserve and World Bank expect a GDP growth rate of 1.4% in 2025, down from 2.8% in 2024. The OECD expects a GDP growth rate of 1.6%. An economic slowdown bodes ill for loan growth.

Fortunately, BankUnited has recently expanded its network, which will likely help with loan growth. As mentioned in the earnings release, the company has launched new wholesale banking offices in Morristown, NJ, and Charlotte, NC.

Considering these factors, I'm expecting the loan book to decline by 0.2% in each of the remaining two quarters in 2025.

Securities' Growth to Partially Compensate for Loan Pressure

BankUnited has been able to grow its deposits at a decent rate so far this year, regardless of the loan trend. The deposit portfolio grew by 2.8% in the first half of this year, or 5.6% annualized. The growth came on the back of an increase in the non-interest-bearing deposits, which was good for the net interest margin.

However, due to the deposit and loan trends, BKU has parked the funds raised through deposits in securities instead of loans, which was not that good for the margin. Doing so led to income forgone. This is because the yields on securities are lower than the yields on loans. As per the details given in the earnings release, the average yield on loans was 5.55%, while the average yield on securities was 5.06% during 2Q'25.

Going forward, deposits will likely continue to grow at a modest rate despite real GDP growth slowdown. This is because nominal deposits will likely grow as inflation heightens due to tariffs. (This article explains the link between inflation and nominal deposits.)

Based on my outlook for loans and deposits, I think BKU will have to park the funds raised from deposits in securities. The shift in the asset mix from loans to securities will have a negative, albeit small, effect on the net interest margin.

The following table shows my balance sheet estimates. Securities make up most of the line item "Other Earning Assets" below.

Falling Deposit Costs to Lift the Margin

BankUnited's net interest margin sprang back up by 12 basis points in 2Q'25 after declining by 3 basis points in 1Q'25. Going forward, there will be an opportunity to further raise the margin by reducing funding costs in the wake of interest rate cuts. The futures market is expecting a 50-basis-point rate cut later this year, as per CME. BankUnited's deposit cost follows the Fed funds rate's trajectory, as shown in the chart below. The close link is attributable to the large balance of interest-bearing demand, savings, and money market deposits, which altogether made up 55% of total deposits at the end of June 2025.

On the other hand, as I discussed above, the asset mix shift will slightly pressurize the average earning-asset yield going forward, which will pressure the margin.

Overall, I'm expecting the net interest margin to grow by 4 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Slashing the Efficiency Ratio and Raising the EPS Estimate

BankUnited's earnings over the last three quarters have been higher than the expectations given in my last report on the company, released in December 2024. The biggest reason for the deviance was that BankUnited has shown more expense discipline than previously anticipated. The efficiency ratio has averaged 61.1% over the last three quarters, down from 64.0% in 3Q'24. I had expected an efficiency ratio of 64.5% for 4Q'24 and full year 2025.

Although non-interest expenses have grown, the efficiency ratio has improved because these expenses have not grown at par with the revenue growth. The sub-category that saw the most improvement was deposit insurance expense. Based on the performance so far, I've decided to reduce my efficiency ratio estimate for 2025 to 61.9% from my previous estimate of 64.5%.

Meanwhile, I'm going to hold my provision expense estimate almost unchanged, as it has been mostly in line with my expectations so far.

For the remainder of the year, I'm expecting slight net interest margin expansion and securities growth to drive earnings. Overall, I'm expecting BankUnited to report an EPS of $3.37 for 2025, up by 10% from last year. In my last report, I gave an EPS estimate of $3.00 for 2025. I've raised my EPS estimate because I've reduced my efficiency ratio estimate.

Upgrading to Hold

In my last report issued in December 2024, I adopted a sell rating on BankUnited. Since then the stock price has dipped by around 8%. To update my valuation, I'm using the historical average price-to-earnings ("P/E") and price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") multiples given in the chart below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $37.9 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $40.8 for the end of 2025.

The average price target for the end of 2025 comes out to $39.4, which is just 1.5% above the July 24 closing market price. BankUnited also offers a moderate dividend yield of 3.1%. Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of 4.6%. Hence, it makes sense to upgrade BankUnited to a hold rating from my previous rating of sell.

data.title: The #1 Mistake High-Yield Investors Keep Making (I Learned The Hard Way)
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola

I have now been a high-yield investor for well over a decade, successfully navigating sectors like MLPs (AMLP), BDCs (BIZD), REITs (VNQ), and CEFs.

Since the launch of our publicly-tracked portfolio at High Yield Investor, we have earned a 16% average annual return, easily beating our high-yield benchmark (DIV), and even outperforming the tech-heavy S&P500 (SPY):

Strategy Annualized Return
HYI Core Portfolio 16.01%
Global X Super Dividend ETF 7.81%
S&P 500 13.78%

But we didn't achieve these results without facing setbacks along the way.

In fact, we have made many mistakes over the years, some of which have cost us a lot of money.

In today's article, I want to give you the chance to learn from my most important mistake ever. Had I known this early in my investing career, I would be a lot richer today.

Yet, I see a lot of income-oriented investors still make this same mistake all the time here on Seeking Alpha.

They will get too greedy and go after ultra-high-yielding stocks offering 10% dividend yields or more.

There are countless articles being posted all the time, pitching these high-yielding opportunities with seemingly sustainable double-digit yields, and it saddens us to see lots of conservative income investors fall for it.

The reality is that if a dividend stock is trading at a 10%+ dividend yield, there is typically a very good reason for it.

The market may not be perfectly efficient, but it is totally stupid either.

If the investment community is collectively pricing a stock at an over 10% dividend yield, there is almost always a good explanation for it.

From our decade-long experience covering this space, I can tell you that most of these companies are overleveraged, own risky assets, and/or suffer from conflicted management, which then ultimately leads to disappointing results over time.

How many times have we seen such ultra-high-yielding stocks get pitched as a "safe dividend investment" just to see their share price collapse and their dividend get cut later on?

It is, unfortunately, very common, and I know this because I have been on the receiving end myself.

This is not to say that every 10%+ yielding stock should be avoided at all costs. There certainly are rare opportunities that may provide a sustainable dividend.

But from our experience, most investors would be much better off if they weren't so greedy for income, and instead focused on somewhat lower-yielding opportunities.

Your goal should not be to maximize income at the expense of growth and safety.

Instead, you should aim to optimize the balance between income, growth, safety, and upside. When you have the right balance, that's when you earn the best returns in the high-yield space, and this study proves it:

It shows clearly that the worst performers are "dividend cutters & eliminators," many of which are those very high-yielding stocks.

On the other hand, the best performers are those that are able to consistently grow their dividends. They are not the highest yielders, but they enjoy consistent growth, which ultimately leads to higher total returns.

The warning here is that, in most cases, investors would be much better off buying a safe and growing 6% yielding stock than a flat and risky 10% yielding stock.

Yet, most individual investors here on Seeking Alpha seem to be a lot more interested in those ultra-high-yielding stocks, and I know this because articles covering such stocks will typically get a lot higher page views:

Let's look at an example to illustrate this point.

About 10 years ago, our analyst Jussi Askola recommended a mall REIT called CBL & Associates (CBL), which back then offered a 10%+ dividend yield. Back then, we thought that the dividend was sustainable because it was safely covered with a low 50% payout ratio, and the assets were producing stable cash flow, despite the headwinds facing the sector. But in hindsight, this was a giant mistake. The company was facing growing capex needs to retain its struggling tenants, and as capex surged, its balance sheet became increasingly dangerous. Eventually, the dividend was cut, and the share price crashed. The company even had to file for Chapter 11 during the pandemic, resulting in a complete loss for common equity holders. (Fortunately, he had exited his investment already before it got so bad, recognizing his initial mistake.)

Now compare that to a safer, steadily growing, high-yielding opportunity. Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) is currently offering a 5.5% dividend yield, but importantly, it has a strong BBB+ rated balance sheet, and it has guided to grow its cash flow (per share) by 10%+ annually going forward. Just between the yield and the growth, investors can expect to earn 15%+ annual return, assuming that its valuation multiple remains intact. But we think there is additional upside potential as the market reprices it at a higher cash flow multiple.

We have bought a lot of shares in recent years at High Yield Investor, with some purchases as low as just $20 per share. We are already up about 32% on those purchases, plus the dividend we have earned along the way.

We would much rather buy BEP-like companies nine times out of ten.

There is no point in earning a 10%+ dividend yield if that comes with a steadily declining share price and an eventual dividend cut.

We have achieved our superior total returns over time by seeking an optimal mix of yield, growth, safety, and upside potential. BEP is a perfect example of that, but there are many more such opportunities. We today hold 16 similar opportunities in our Core Portfolio, most of which offer a dividend yield in the 5-8% range.

We also own a few 10%+ yielding opportunities at High Yield Investor, but those are exceptions. We certainly wouldn't build our entire strategy around such risky, high-yielding companies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
